content: Kelly Criterion: f* = (bp - q) / b, where f* = fraction of capital, b = reward-to-risk ratio, p = win probability, q = 1-p. Maximizes long-term geometric growth. Use Half Kelly (f*/2) in practice — same growth direction with 75% less variance.

Value at Risk (VaR): 95% VaR = 1.645 * sigma * sqrt(T), 99% VaR = 2.326 * sigma * sqrt(T). Three methods: parametric (assumes normal), historical (uses actual returns), Monte Carlo (simulates paths). Historical VaR captures fat tails that parametric misses.

CVaR (Expected Shortfall) is the average loss beyond VaR threshold. More informative than VaR because it measures tail severity, not just probability. CVaR at 95% = E[Loss | Loss > VaR_95]. Preferred by risk managers over VaR for portfolio optimization.

Sharpe Ratio = (Rp - Rf) / sigma_p. Annualize daily Sharpe by multiplying by sqrt(252). Thresholds: <1.0 suboptimal, 1.0-1.99 good, 2.0-2.99 very good, >3.0 excellent. Context matters: leveraged strategies inflate volatility and reduce Sharpe.

Sortino Ratio = (Rp - Rf) / sigma_downside. Unlike Sharpe which penalizes all volatility, Sortino ignores upside vol. A Sortino of 2.0+ indicates strong downside risk management. Preferred for asymmetric return strategies like options selling.

Calmar Ratio = Annualized Return / Maximum Drawdown. Fund with 30% return and 15% max drawdown = Calmar 2.0 (good). Above 1.0 acceptable, above 3.0 excellent. Best metric for evaluating hedge funds and active trading strategies.

Maximum Drawdown = (Trough - Peak) / Peak as percentage. Track running peak equity continuously. Drawdown exceeding 20% signals strategy review. Exceeding 40% indicates potential system failure. Recovery time often exceeds drawdown duration by 2-3x.

Position sizing rule: risk 1-2% of portfolio per trade. Calculate: position_size = (Account * Risk%) / (Entry - StopLoss). Example: $100K account, 1% risk ($1000), entry $50, stop $48 = 500 shares. Never size based on conviction alone.

Correlation tracking: if positions correlate >0.7, they're effectively the same bet. Long NVDA+AMD+SMCI+SOXL = 4x semiconductor exposure. Track rolling 30-day correlation matrix across all positions. Limit correlated exposure to 15% of portfolio.

Options Delta: rate of change of option price per $1 move in underlying. ATM call Delta ≈ 0.50, deep ITM ≈ 1.0, far OTM ≈ 0. Put Delta is negative. Portfolio Delta = sum of all position Deltas. Delta-neutral = total Delta ≈ 0.

Options Gamma: rate of change of Delta per $1 move. Gamma = d²C/dS². Highest for ATM options near expiration. Long Gamma profits from large moves (straddles). Short Gamma profits from stagnation but has unlimited risk on big moves.

Options Theta: time decay per day. Theta = dC/dt. ATM options decay fastest. Theta accelerates in final 30 days (square root of time rule). Option sellers collect Theta but bear Gamma risk. Weekly options have highest Theta but highest Gamma.

Options Vega: sensitivity to 1% change in implied volatility. Vega = dC/d(sigma). Longer-dated options have higher Vega. Buy options (long Vega) before expected volatility events. Sell options (short Vega) when IV is elevated above historical realized vol.

Black-Scholes: C = S*N(d1) - K*e^(-rT)*N(d2), where d1 = [ln(S/K) + (r + sigma²/2)*T] / (sigma*sqrt(T)), d2 = d1 - sigma*sqrt(T). Assumes constant volatility and log-normal returns. Foundation for all options pricing despite known limitations.

Put-Call Parity: C - P = S - K*e^(-rT). If violated, arbitrage exists. Use to derive put price from call price (or vice versa). Also validates options quotes — significant parity violations indicate stale or erroneous pricing.

Covered call: long 100 shares + short 1 OTM call. Generates income from premium, caps upside at strike. Best in sideways/slightly bullish markets. Yield: sell 30-delta calls 30-45 DTE for ~2-3% monthly premium on stock value.

Iron condor: sell OTM call spread + sell OTM put spread. Profits from low volatility (stock stays between short strikes). Max profit = net credit. Max loss = width of wider spread - credit. Target 70%+ probability of profit, close at 50% of max profit.

Straddle: buy ATM call + ATM put same strike/expiration. Profits from large moves in either direction. Cost = call premium + put premium. Breakeven = strike ± total premium. Buy before binary events (earnings, FDA), sell when IV is elevated.

Butterfly spread: buy 1 low strike + sell 2 middle strike + buy 1 high strike (all calls or puts). Low cost, limited risk, profits when stock pins to middle strike at expiration. Max profit at middle strike, max loss = net debit.

IV Rank: current IV as percentile of IV over past year. IV Rank > 50 = elevated vol (favor selling options). IV Rank < 30 = low vol (favor buying options). IV Percentile shows what % of days IV was lower — more robust than IV Rank.

Drawdown-based position reduction: 0-5% drawdown = full position size, 5-10% = half size, 10-15% = quarter size, 15%+ = pause all new trades. This graduated approach prevents catastrophic losses while allowing recovery without full shutdown.

ATR-based stop placement: stop = entry - (ATR_14 * multiplier). Multiplier of 2x ATR for swing trades, 3x for trend following. ATR adapts to current volatility automatically — tight stops in calm markets, wide stops in volatile markets.

Circuit breakers for trading systems: VIX > 30 = reduce all position sizes 50%. VIX > 40 = halt new positions. 3 consecutive losing trades = pause 24 hours. Daily loss > 3% = stop trading for the day. Weekly loss > 5% = review all strategies.

Monte Carlo simulation for portfolio risk: simulate 10,000+ random return paths using historical mean/std. Measure: probability of ruin, expected max drawdown, VaR at various confidence levels. Use to stress-test before deploying capital.

Risk parity allocates portfolio weights so each position contributes equal risk: w_i * sigma_i * corr(i, portfolio) = constant. Unlike equal weight, risk parity accounts for volatility differences. Bonds get higher weight than stocks due to lower vol.

Hierarchical Risk Parity (HRP): uses hierarchical clustering on correlation matrix to build a tree, then allocates risk top-down through the hierarchy. Outperforms Markowitz mean-variance in out-of-sample tests by avoiding matrix inversion instability.

Markowitz mean-variance optimization: min w^T*Sigma*w subject to w^T*mu = target_return, w^T*1 = 1. The efficient frontier traces optimal risk-return tradeoffs. Practical issue: extremely sensitive to estimation errors in expected returns.

Black-Litterman model: mu_BL = [(tau*Sigma)^(-1) + P^T*Omega^(-1)*P]^(-1) * [(tau*Sigma)^(-1)*pi + P^T*Omega^(-1)*q]. Combines market equilibrium (pi) with investor views (P,q). More stable than pure Markowitz because it anchors to equilibrium.

Tail risk hedging: buy 5-10% OTM puts on SPY (2-3 month expiry) sized at 1-2% of portfolio value. VIX calls (strike 30-40) provide convex payoff during crashes. Total cost target: 0.5-1% of portfolio annually for crash insurance.

Portfolio beta hedging: calculate portfolio beta vs S&P 500, then short SPY futures or buy SPY puts to neutralize. Hedge ratio = portfolio_value * portfolio_beta / SPY_price. Rebalance monthly as betas change.

Slippage monitoring: track difference between expected fill price and actual fill. Average slippage > 0.1% on liquid stocks indicates execution quality issues. High slippage on specific names may indicate low liquidity or adverse selection.

Mean reversion z-score: Z = (spread - mean) / std_dev. Standard entry: |Z| >= 2.0. Standard exit: |Z| <= 0.5. Stop-loss: |Z| >= 3.0. Research shows optimized thresholds of entry=1.42, exit=0.37 can outperform conventional 2.0/1.0.

Pairs trading cointegration: test with Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test. p-value < 0.05 = mean-reverting spread. Calculate half-life: t_half = -ln(2)/ln(beta) where beta = AR(1) coefficient. Profitable half-lives are 1-30 days; beyond 60 = too slow.

Momentum factor construction (Jegadeesh-Titman): rank universe by returns from t-12 to t-2 months (skip most recent month). Buy top decile, sell bottom decile. Skip month avoids short-term reversal. Yields approximately 1% per month historically.

AI trading benchmarks (2025): ML frameworks achieving Sharpe > 2.5, max drawdown ~3%, near-zero S&P 500 correlation. Gold futures: Sharpe 2.88, CAGR 2.65%, max drawdown 0.52%. Key: ML signals + Kelly sizing + friction-adjusted optimization.

VWAP execution: split orders proportional to historical volume patterns across time intervals. 74% of hedge funds use VWAP. TWAP distributes evenly regardless of volume, better for unpredictable volume or illiquid instruments.

Short interest ratio = short shares / average daily volume = days to cover. Ratio > 10 = high squeeze potential. Monitor short interest changes: rapid increases signal bearish conviction, rapid decreases may signal short covering (bullish).

Strategy-level risk controls: track Sharpe ratio per strategy in real-time. Auto-disable strategies with Sharpe < 0 over rolling 30-day window. Implement weight decay: reduce allocation to underperforming strategies by 10% per losing week.

Sector concentration risk: limit any single sector to 25% of portfolio. GICS sectors: Technology, Healthcare, Financials, Consumer Discretionary, Industrials, Communication Services, Consumer Staples, Energy, Utilities, Materials, Real Estate.

Liquidity risk: never take a position larger than 1% of average daily volume. For small-cap stocks (< $500M market cap), reduce to 0.5% ADV. Illiquid positions can't be exited quickly during drawdowns, amplifying losses.

Overnight gap risk: holding positions overnight exposes to gap openings from after-hours news. Reduce position sizes on stocks reporting earnings. Use options (straddles) instead of directional stock positions around binary events.

Crypto-specific risks: smart contract exploits (TVL can go to zero), oracle manipulation (flash loan attacks), regulatory risk (exchange delistings, SEC actions), impermanent loss in AMM pools, bridge hacks. Never risk more than 5% of total portfolio in any single DeFi protocol.

Impermanent loss in AMM liquidity pools: IL = 2*sqrt(price_ratio)/(1+price_ratio) - 1. At 2x price move: -5.7% IL. At 5x: -25.5%. Only provide liquidity in correlated pairs (ETH/stETH) or stablecoin pairs (USDC/USDT) to minimize IL.

Risk-adjusted position sizing with volatility scaling: target_vol = 15% annual. Position_weight = target_vol / (asset_vol * sqrt(252)). This automatically reduces positions in volatile assets and increases in stable ones, maintaining consistent portfolio risk.

Correlation breakdown during crises: asset correlations spike toward 1.0 during market stress (2008: cross-asset correlation rose from 0.3 to 0.8+). Diversification fails when you need it most. Hedge with truly uncorrelated assets: gold, long volatility, managed futures.

Trade journal requirements: record entry/exit prices, position size, entry rationale, strategy name, market conditions, emotional state, actual vs expected P&L. Review weekly. This creates the feedback loop for continuous strategy improvement.

Win rate vs risk-reward tradeoff: a strategy with 40% win rate needs >1.5:1 reward:risk to be profitable. 60% win rate needs only 1:1. Kelly optimal sizing considers both: higher win rates allow larger positions. Track both metrics per strategy.

Drawdown recovery math: -10% loss requires +11.1% gain to recover. -20% requires +25%. -50% requires +100%. -80% requires +400%. This asymmetry makes drawdown prevention far more important than return maximization.

Riskfolio-Lib provides portfolio optimization in Python: mean-variance, risk parity, CVaR, Black-Litterman, HRP, efficient frontier. Built on CVXPY. pip install riskfolio-lib. Integrates with pandas DataFrames of returns.

VectorBT enables lightning-fast vectorized backtesting: tests thousands of parameter combinations in seconds using NumPy/Numba. Compare strategies via Sharpe, max drawdown, win rate. pip install vectorbt. Essential for ROOT's strategy validation before live trading.
